⚽ Ten qualities that set a good coach apart from one who really leaves a mark on their players. Beyond the tactical system and the whiteboard, something makes the difference: knowing how to listen, building a bond and managing the dressing room well. Drawn from F. Ducasse's book «The Coach's and Performer's Toolkit», which lays out around twenty essential qualities, here are the ten that caught our attention. How many do you already master?

⚽ Determination, emotional control, discipline, clear goals... the qualities that push players and coaches to perform. Many of them are shared by everyone who succeeds, whether in sport, in business or in any other field. Here are ten that footballers, and their coaches, need to reach the top, with success understood in the sense of real performance. Which ones already define you?

⚽ Agility is a decisive quality in modern football: starts, stops, changes of direction and of pace show up constantly throughout a match. Get it wrong and you're always half a step late. In this piece you'll see what agility really is, why it matters so much, and when and how to work on it with your players, plus six exercises ready to take straight into your session.

⚽ The ten key factors that shape a young footballer's development over the years, not over a single season. LTPD, Long-Term Player Development, is a framework that organises a player's growth for the long run. Here we share the ten factors drawn from Canada's «Long-Term Athlete Development plan», a global reference adapted to football, so you can guide your youngsters without burning them out. Which ones are you already applying?

⚽ Strength and running in the same session: that's how strength-interval work operates. Interval training alternates short, intense efforts with recovery periods, or low-intensity activity, to develop maximal aerobic power (MAP). Here's the key: it doesn't all have to be running. You can build strength exercises straight into the interval work. We share Cometti's progression, the effort-recovery times and 12 ready-to-use exercises for your next session.

⚽ Movement frequency is one of the three parameters that, since Zatsiorsky, make up the quality of speed. In football every component counts: develop each one a little and you raise the overall level. Speed is largely genetic, you won't turn a very slow player into a very fast one, but it stays trainable and small gains have big effects. Here you'll see what movement frequency is, when to work on it and six groups of ready-to-use exercises.

⚽ Twenty-two physical exercises you can run with just a few flat markers, cones or hoops. In amateur football the kit available for physical work is often limited, but that's no excuse to stand still. With the little equipment any club can get hold of, you can set up plenty of effective, fun drills covering warm-up, speed, endurance and coordination, suitable for any team. Which one will you try first?

⚽ Twenty-five small-sided games to train the physical, technical and tactical sides all at once. A quick reminder: small-sided games are all the games with opposition played with reduced numbers, such as 2v2, 4v4, 6v6 or 8v4. Played with a ball and opposition, they let you build endurance, technique and tactics in a single format. You'll find each one laid out with diagrams and every variation explained, ready for your session.

⚽ Five exercises to sharpen your players' reaction and decision speed. Since Zatsiorsky we've known that the quality of speed involves three distinct parameters, reaction time, movement speed and movement frequency, each one worked separately and specifically. Here we focus on reaction: how fast a player reads a cue and gets moving. You'll find the five exercises laid out with diagrams, ready to take into your next session.

⚽ You can build real strength without a gym or professional kit. Amateur clubs live with two big constraints: little time, two or three sessions a week, and little equipment, no weights room or pro machines. But strength work stays essential in football, even at amateur level. Here you'll see five ways to train it within reach of any club: circuits, stairs, hills, plyometrics and bodyweight work.

⚽ Within a solid training methodology there's one stage you shouldn't skip: the testing phase. It's often neglected, yet it hands you and your players genuinely valuable information, where they stand, what to prioritise and whether your work is paying off. Here are five reasons to assess your players and, above all, how to make the most of what the results tell you.

⚽ Pre-season is far more than a simple return to the pitch: it shapes your entire season. It's the key window for laying the physical, tactical and mental foundations of the whole year, and a well-run preparation can transform how your campaign unfolds, as long as you don't improvise. Here are the six essential points to get your team ready on every front, with a clear plan rather than guesswork.

⚽ Sleep is the number one recovery tool, ahead of massages, compression, cold baths or stretching. Many specialists agree on it: if you don't sleep well, no other recovery strategy can make up for the gap. It's during sleep that the body truly repairs and adapts. Here are seven tips to sleep better, recover more and, as a result, get injured less across the season.

⚽ Progression, continuity, variety, alternation, specificity, individualisation and effective load: the seven principles that govern all effective training. Training is a way of improving physical qualities and performance, but training time is limited, so you have to use it well and get the most out of it. That means planning and programming your sessions. And to deliver physical work that actually pays off, you lean on these great principles.

⚽ The coordination ladder, first used in athletics, is now a fixture in many football coaches' sessions. It brings real advantages, foot speed, rhythm and coordination, but it also has clear limits that are easy to overlook. Used well it adds value; used badly it just fills time. Here are seven reasons to use it and, just as important, the mistakes to avoid with your players.

⚽ Confidence isn't innate: it's built through work and experience. A lack of it is common at every level, yet believing in yourself is fundamental. Confidence lets you dare, take risks, make decisions and face a match calmly, whether you're dribbling, playing out from the back or shooting from distance. Without it, a player just «clears it», which rarely leads to a goal. Here's where confidence comes from, the coach's role and eight exercises to strengthen it.

⚽ There's no blueprint for the perfect leader, but there are qualities the great ones share. According to many management and leadership specialists, Pierre Cabane among them, in sport as in business, no single mould produces a leader. What you can identify are the characteristics common to all the captains who genuinely make the difference. Here are the ten every good captain should cultivate. Which ones do you already recognise in your dressing room?

⚽ Speed is one of the most decisive qualities in football, and one of the worst trained. We tend to reduce it to the classic straight-line sprint, when in reality it's far richer than that. Here you'll see what speed really is and how to build a session step by step: warm-up, the sequences themselves, the recoveries to apply between them and how to plan it all across the season.

⚽ Power is the combination of strength and speed, and it underpins every duel, jump, sprint and strike. You could say it's strength expressed at speed: powerful players apply a lot of force even when moving very fast. Exercises can lean towards speed (speed-power) or towards strength (strength-power) depending on your player. Here you'll discover your profile and the methods to develop the power that decides those key actions.

⚽ The hamstrings account for most muscle injuries in football, so it pays to understand them before you train them. Before looking at why and how to develop them, it's worth grasping their anatomy and their role in the game: braking, sprinting, protecting the knee. Here we explain their real importance and offer you concrete methods to work on them, so your players get injured less and perform more.

⚽ Confidence isn't innate: it's built, fed and, just as easily, destroyed. As a coach you have a direct hand in it, in your words, your feedback and the way you set up your sessions. A player who trusts themselves dares more, decides faster and plays with freedom. Here you'll see why confidence changes everything and six concrete ideas to reinforce it, session after session, in your group.

⚽ Do you really have to suffer to gain flexibility? It's a very widespread belief, but pain doesn't have to go hand in hand with stretching. Understanding how your protocols work and staying consistent lets you make real progress safely, without gritting your teeth. Here you'll see how to gain range without the hard time, and why steady, well-run stretching beats forcing it every time.

⚽ Every player has their own MAS, so a one-size-fits-all interval drill leaves some working too hard and others coasting. To develop aerobic power, one of the favourite methods is interval work such as «30-30» or «15-15» at paces close to each player's MAS. Here you'll learn how to work out the exact distance for those runs, so you can individualise the work right there on the pitch.

⚽ A complete stretching routine for the lower body, ready to use after training or a match. These stretches let the muscles recover their initial resting length, counter the tendency to stiffen and help the joints regain their mobility. Done consistently at the right moment, they support recovery and steady flexibility gains. Here's the full routine, exercise by exercise, so you can slot it straight into your players' cool-down.

⚽ Interval work improves VO2max more effectively than continuous running, which makes it one of the best tools for building condition in futsal. It's a sport of short, intense, repeated efforts, so the way you train it should look the same. Here we explain what interval work involves and what it's for, and share six ready-to-use futsal exercises, runs, strength, reaction and small-sided games, to vary every session.

⚽ What if grouping your players by their biological maturity, rather than their birth date, helped them progress more? Youngsters usually train and play with others their own age, convenient for the club, but not always ideal for their development. Within a single age group, differences in growth and psychological maturity create real inequalities. Here you'll discover bio-banding, what it changes and how to apply it with your young players.

⚽ The warm-up is a key phase of match preparation, with psychological, technical and physiological sides, and two clear jobs: preventing injuries and preparing performance. The aim is to raise muscle temperature towards 39°, the point where the body is ready to perform. Here you'll understand what actually happens in the body during the warm-up, why the «Russian» bottom-up method is the most effective, and how to organise it before a match and before a session.

⚽ Circuit training is one of the most versatile tools you have: it lets you work several physical qualities in a single session, inject real dynamism into training and keep full control over the group. Whatever your equipment or your numbers, there's a circuit that fits. Here you'll see why to use it, when it works best, how to build it and which example circuits you can take straight into your session.

⚽ You can have the perfect exercises, the right formats and a spotless set-up, and still see the physical work fail to happen. Your players run, sweat and finish exhausted, yet the quality you wanted to develop may never really appear. Why? Because between what you plan and what the players produce there's a gap, and your words are what closes it or widens it. Here's how your coaching talk truly steers the physical work.

⚽ Fartlek is one of those methods that look simple but hide a lot of potential. That «speed game» born in Sweden is fun, free and highly adaptable, an excellent way to build your players' endurance away from the stadium. Here I explain where it comes from, what its advantages and drawbacks are, its different forms and, above all, how to programme it across the season with your amateur team.

⚽ The core is much more than the classic static plank. It's the foundation on which your players produce and transmit force in every action of a match, from a sprint to a duel to a strike, and it's what protects the back along the way. Here you'll see what core work really is, why it matters so much in football and how to train it effectively, at any age.

⚽ Should you work endurance with or without the ball? The question keeps coming back. Some swear by running and interval work; others run their players into the ground through small-sided games. The truth is more nuanced: both routes have their pros and cons. The real challenge isn't picking one over the other, but knowing when and why to use each. Here you'll see how to combine them with your team.

⚽ The ankle is the joint most exposed to sprains in football: complex, very mobile and heavily taxed in every stride, cut and landing. It's no surprise that a large share of the injuries in the game are ankle sprains. Here you'll understand why it happens, how to heal it properly so you don't come back too soon, and how to prevent it from turning into a recurring problem.

⚽ Does stretching before a match really reduce your strength? True or false? In recent years some studies have shown that stretching before playing can cut the strength of the muscles you stretch, and many jumped to the conclusion that you should drop stretching from the warm-up altogether. The answer, though, depends entirely on the type of stretching. Here you'll discover which method genuinely belongs in your warm-up, and which one doesn't.

⚽ Should you stretch after exercise? A physiotherapist for the France national team answers clearly: yes, you should. The debate has been poisoned for years, mostly because those who dismissed post-exercise stretching never took the full range of methods into account. Here we lean on the routine shared by C. Geoffroy, a stretching specialist, to lay out the golden rules, the protocol to follow and the mistakes to avoid.

⚽ The skipping rope is one of the most complete and cheapest tools a footballer has: cardio, explosiveness, coordination, ankle strength and footwork, all in a single action. It's versatile, easy to carry and costs almost nothing, you can even make one yourself from climbing rope, and you can use it just about anywhere. Here's everything you need to know: why, when and how to bring it into your work.

⚽ The way you mentally manage an injury shapes both the speed and the quality of your recovery. When you get hurt you look for the external cause, but sometimes the cause is closer to home, within yourself: several psychological factors can quietly make injuries more likely. Here you'll see how to face an injury without letting it break you, and how to turn a forced pause into a real opportunity.

⚽ Technique is essential to strike the ball harder, but it has its limits. Many coaches miss the mark by working only on technique, when power matters just as much: power = force × distance / time. It's the same reason stronger weightlifters run faster and jump higher. Once the technique is in place, you raise striking power by getting stronger and more explosive. Here are seven ways to do exactly that, from German coach Moritz Klatten.

⚽ A drop of just 1% in hydration can cut your performance by around 10%, and it's one of the easiest things to get right. Your body is more than 60% water, and it isn't a closed circuit: the losses you take on every day have to be replaced with regular, sufficient intake. Here you'll learn when to drink, how much and what, so you perform better, get injured less and recover more.

⚽ Football is an intermittent sport: repeated explosive efforts followed by recovery phases, over and over across ninety minutes. Once you analyse the demands of a match, it becomes clear that your conditioning work should mirror that same rhythm rather than long, steady running. Here you'll learn how to design an interval session built on the real match ratio (around 1/8), so your players are trained for the way the game is actually played.

⚽ Should your young footballers stretch? The practice was questioned a few years ago, when some studies suggested that stretching before matches could reduce strength and performance. Flexibility got caught in the crossfire, and many concluded it wasn't worth working on at all. But young players have their own needs, and the answer depends on the type of stretching and the age. Here's the guide to knowing how and when yours should stretch.

⚽ For many coaches, D. Deschamps and D. Simeone among them, the essential thing is to build a group before anything else. Whether you're a player or a coach, the question of cohesion surely speaks to you, because a united dressing room often carries a team further than talent alone. Here you'll see what cohesion really is, what it's for and, above all, how to strengthen it within your own team.

⚽ Eating well gives you energy, speeds up recovery and lowers your risk of injury, it's one of the real keys to performing better and staying healthy all season. Yet it's often the piece players leave to chance. Here we share the INSEP's advice and practical tips to build the footballer's ideal meal: what to put on your plate, in what balance and why. Small changes here pay off week after week.

⚽ For a young player to develop in the best possible way, there's a set of principles and growth phases you need to respect, not chronological age alone, but biological age, the golden ages and the natural stages of development. Rush them and you risk burning them out; guide them well and you give them a real long-term future. Here you'll see how to develop your young footballers with a genuine long-term vision.

⚽ Plyometrics is one of the most effective ways to develop explosiveness and functional strength in football, the jumps, sprints and changes of direction that decide actions. But dosed badly, it can also cause injury. Here you'll see exactly why it works, when to introduce it into the training week and how to control the volume, so you improve your players' explosiveness without breaking them along the way.

⚽ Proprioception is your body's internal GPS: it prevents injuries, improves joint locking and sharpens coordination. It's one of those areas too often neglected in amateur football, and yet it makes a real difference both in staying fit and in performing. Here you'll understand exactly what it is, why you should develop it, with what progression and methods to work on it, and when and with whom to bring it into your sessions.

⚽ Mental recovery matters as much as the training itself. Recovery is the state in which the body returns to its baseline after an effort, letting you regenerate, adapt and, in turn, progress, and it applies to the mind just as much as the muscles. Here you'll see why you get mentally tired over a season and which methods genuinely help you switch off and recover, whether you're an elite player or an amateur.

⚽ Every physical quality has its ideal moment to be worked on, its golden age. In the development of young players it's essential to take a long-term view, respecting the natural phases of growth and the specific needs that come with each one. Push a quality too early or too late and you miss the window. Here you'll discover the golden ages and what to prioritise according to the age of your youngsters.

⚽ The first actions after a muscle tear or a sprain shape your entire recovery. Handle those first few minutes badly and you can stretch the time out by weeks; handle them well and you save yourself a lot of them. Here you'll learn the POLICE protocol, how to run a quick assessment right there on the pitch and how to manage the full care that follows, so your players come back sooner and safer.

⚽ Cramp, muscle strain, pulled muscle, tear: they get lumped together, but they're not the same thing, and telling them apart changes how you treat them. Muscle injury is one of the most common enemies of every footballer, professional or amateur. Here you'll understand the different types, how to treat each one with the POLICE protocol and, above all, how to prevent them so your players don't keep relapsing.

⚽ Small-sided games work the physical, technical, tactical and mental sides all at once, which is what makes them so valuable. A quick reminder: they're all the games with opposition played with reduced numbers, 2v2, 4v4, 6v6 and so on. The real skill is designing them so they train exactly what you want. Here you'll see why they work and how to build your own, with sixteen practical examples to get you started.

⚽ The amateur summer break lasts about ten weeks, and it's usually spent just «keeping fit» with slow, long jogs so you don't come back too detrained. But what if you used that time to genuinely improve instead of merely maintaining? Ten weeks is long enough to build something real. Here you'll see how to turn the break into a window for progress, not just survival, and arrive sharper than when you left.

⚽ How is it that players who are sometimes shorter and lighter still win most of their aerial duels and score plenty of headed goals? It's rarely just height, it's the ability to jump well and, above all, to stabilise yourself in mid-air. Here you'll learn how to improve your vertical jump and hold your position in the air, so you win more duels and head the ball with real power.

⚽ A hard physical session, well dosed, does more than raise fitness: shared effort also builds group cohesion. Cohesion is regularly named as one of the keys to performance, and several studies show a genuine link between the two. Suffering together, pushing through and coming out the other side welds a group in a way few things can. Here you'll see how physical work can reinforce the mental side and the unity of your team.

⚽ Do you carry a flexibility deficit in certain muscles, hamstrings, quadriceps, calves, or a mobility deficit in a joint like the hip or shoulder? Long passive stretches, the plastic phase of the tissue and C. Geoffroy's protocol are the tools that turn that around. Here you'll see how to spot the deficit, why it's worth improving and, above all, how to work on flexibility effectively to gain range, prevent injuries and perform more.

⚽ Only around 5% of the explosive actions in a match actually decide it, and those are the moments where everything is at stake. It was from this very observation that Gilles Cometti built his method for training physical qualities. But in those decisive instants the body alone isn't enough: the mind is what tips them your way. Here you'll see why the physical falls short on its own and how the mental side makes the difference when it counts most.

⚽ Can young players do plyometrics? For years it was thought dangerous for growth, and the idea still carries a lot of baggage. Is it really risky? From what age can you start, and with what heights and what dosage? Today the science is clear: dosed properly, plyometrics is both safe and effective in youngsters. Here you'll see what the studies actually say and how to work on it safely and progressively with your players.

⚽ You've surely seen it: a player who delivers a strong first half and disappears in the second. He isn't injured or unmotivated, he's simply empty. That's a question of aerobic power, and it's trainable. Here you'll find a clear theory of endurance, VO2max and interval work, plus five detailed sessions, from U15 to adults, so your players are still there in the ninetieth minute rather than running on fumes.

⚽ The summer pre-season is a key period: get it right and you set up your whole season, get it wrong and you spend months paying for it. It's easy to fall into the usual traps, doing too much too soon, neglecting recovery or improvising the load. Here are seven tips and the mistakes to avoid, so you prepare your team sensibly and start the season on the right foot.

⚽ Dissociated, associated or integrated: these are the three main ways of working on the physical side in football, and each one has its place. You may already have come across the terms; if not, the difference comes down to how much the ball and the game are woven into the physical work. Here's a clear explanation of all three, with examples, their advantages, their limits and when to use each one with your team.

⚽ The last meal before a match is one of the real factors in your performance. Get it right and you feel light and full of energy on the pitch; get it wrong and you can undo a whole week's work, heaviness, flat legs, poor sensations. It's a small decision with a big impact. Here you'll learn what to eat before playing, how long beforehand, and which foods are better left off the plate.

⚽ After an intense match, what you put on your plate decides whether you actually recover or not. The goal is simple: give the body what it needs to rebuild, and those needs aren't the same as before or during the effort. There's a key window, the first 30 minutes, and then the hours that follow. Here you'll find out what to take in and when, so you recover fully and keep progressing.

⚽ After the winter break your players often come back tired and out of shape, and it's easy to just pick up where you left off. By December, bodies are at their limit: heavy pitches, less daylight, months of accumulated load. Then comes the Christmas stop, seven to ten days where most players switch off completely, which is healthy. Here you'll see why a mini pre-season in January can transform your second half of the season.

⚽ What physical work should you do the day before a match? The priority of that last session is simple: prepare for the game, not develop qualities. It's no longer the moment to improve endurance, strength or speed, but to get your players ready to perform on match day. Here you'll see how to arrive fresh and switched on, sharp in the legs and in the head, rather than flat or overloaded.

⚽ Cold, heat, contrast showers, cryotherapy: temperature is a powerful lever for recovery when you know how to use it. Here we look at «medium-term» recovery, the kind that happens between two training sessions, or between a match and the next session. Used well, it helps your players turn up fresher for the following effort. Here you'll see which methods to apply, when and how, to recover better between sessions.

⚽ How you spread physical work across the week and within each session largely decides whether your players peak for Sunday. It depends on several parameters: the number of sessions in the week, the period of the season, the level of your players, their needs and your objectives. Here you'll learn how to distribute each quality by the day and the moment of the session, with the 72-hour rule, so your team arrives ready for the match.

⚽ Which MAS test really suits football: continuous or interval, straight line or shuttle? On paper a player's activity looks like an effort with a strong aerobic dominance, several kilometres covered over more than an hour, but look closer and it's actually a sum of short, intense efforts split by low-intensity recovery. That changes which test makes sense. Here you'll see why Buchheit's 30-15 is king for football, and how to use it.

⚽ For speed work to be effective, recovery is everything. Maximum intensity is the non-negotiable condition, and that has direct consequences on how long your players rest, between each sprint, between sets and between reps. Cut the recovery short and you're no longer training speed, you're training something else. Here you'll see exactly how much rest your players need at every level of the session, so each effort stays truly maximal.

⚽ In amateur football time is tight, so the question comes up: should you prioritise the aerobic side or speed? We tend to work both «naturally» because they're essential, but sometimes you have to choose, train your strengths for small gains, or your weaknesses for bigger ones. Here you'll see how to think it through and how to chase small speed gains that have big effects on the pitch.

⚽ For years, building strength in young players was thought to be dangerous. Today the science says the opposite: done properly, youngsters can and should train strength to develop to their full potential. Skip it and, I promise you, you'll run into trouble down the line, more injuries and a player who plateaus. Here you'll see why strength work belongs in a young footballer's development and how to introduce it the right way.

⚽ The uphill sprint is one of the most accessible and complete resisted speed exercises there is. Training speed with resisted or overloaded work, and running up a slope is exactly that, forces the body to produce more, and that's where the gains come from. It needs no special kit, just a hill. Here you'll see how to train it properly and all the benefits it brings your players' speed and power.

⚽ Downhill running is over-speed work: it breaks the speed barrier and lifts your stride frequency by letting the body move faster than it can on the flat. To keep improving, it's worth varying the work rather than always doing the same sprints, that's how you «surprise» the body and trigger new adaptations, and new adaptations mean progress. Here you'll see how to apply downhill work with no equipment and no unnecessary risks.

⚽ MAS, MAP and VO2max get thrown around every day, in sessions and in programmes, yet they're often used without being fully understood. Whether you're a player or a coach, you've surely worked «at MAS» or «to develop VO2max» without pinning down what each one really means. Here I explain, simply, what each one is, how they relate to one another and why, in football, MAS is the one that truly counts.

⚽ Speed in football isn't just about the straight line. A player can be quick in a straight sprint, react fast, have great movement speed, or shine in their changes of direction, and these aren't the same thing. A player who's fast in a straight line can lose a lot of speed when he has to turn, and linear work alone won't fix it. Here you'll see what agility is, how to test it and how to improve your players' changes of direction.

⚽ What exactly are MAP and MAS? What are the energy systems, and what role does lactic acid really play? These are among the questions we get asked most, and the answers are often tangled up in jargon. Here we explain it all simply, but rigorously: what each concept means, how the energy systems work and why lactic acid isn't quite the villain it's made out to be. Clear ideas you can actually use.
